title: Selecting a DR Strategy from RTO/RPO
description:
## Meeting a Strict RTO/RPO Within Budget

A financial-services company must define cross-Region disaster recovery for its core trading-support application. The business sets an **RTO of about 5 minutes** and an **RPO of seconds (near-zero data loss)**. They are **willing to pay** for standing DR infrastructure but want to **avoid the cost and operational complexity of running full production capacity in both Regions** if they don't have to. Database changes are continuously replicated to the DR Region.

Which DR strategy BEST meets these requirements?

options.text: Backup & Restore — copy nightly snapshots to the DR Region and rebuild on disaster
Pilot Light — keep only the database running in the DR Region and provision the rest on disaster
Warm Standby — run a scaled-down but fully functional copy of the stack in the DR Region and scale it up on failover
Multi-Site Active/Active — run full production capacity serving live traffic in both Regions

explanation: An RTO of minutes with near-zero RPO, where the team wants to avoid paying for full duplicate capacity, is the textbook definition of **Warm Standby**: a smaller-but-complete copy of the workload is always running in the DR Region (so recovery is just scaling up and shifting traffic — minutes), and continuous data replication keeps RPO to seconds. **Backup & Restore** (A) yields RTO/RPO of hours — far too slow. **Pilot Light** (B) keeps only the data hot and must spin up the application tier on disaster, typically giving RTO in the tens of minutes, which misses the ~5-minute target and is riskier under pressure. **Active/Active** (D) would also satisfy the RTO/RPO but contradicts the explicit requirement to avoid running full production capacity in both Regions — it is more cost and complexity than needed.

hints.title: Anchor on the constraints
hints.body: RTO ~minutes + RPO ~seconds rules out Backup & Restore (hours). The phrase "avoid full capacity in both Regions" rules out Active/Active.

hints.title: Pilot Light vs Warm Standby
hints.body: Pilot Light keeps only the core (data) hot and builds the rest on demand; Warm Standby keeps the whole stack running, just smaller. Which gives a faster, lower-risk recovery in ~5 minutes?
